verses.text: Then they told David, saying, Behold, the Philistines fight against Keilah, and they rob the threshingfloors. Therefore David enquired of the LORD, saying, Shall I go and smite these Philistines? And the LORD said unto David, Go, and smite the Philistines, and save Keilah. Guidance: News reaches David's wilderness camp: Philistine raiders are looting the threshing floors at Keilah (a fortified town about three miles south of Adullam) during the spring harvest, when grain was at its most vulnerable. Keilah is not David's town; it is not even in his immediate territory of need. But it is Israelite, and it is being plundered. David's instinct is striking — he asks God whether to intervene. The man whom Saul calls a rebel is in fact the one acting like a king: a Spirit-anointed leader who turns to the LORD rather than to his own appetites for the next move. The inquiry probably uses Abiathar's ephod (just arrived in chapter 22; the Urim and Thummim oracle gave yes/no answers). The LORD answers: 'Go, and smite the Philistines, and save Keilah.' Note that David, in exile and outlawed, is being commissioned by God to do what Saul, the official king of Israel, is not doing. The throne God has chosen is already at work, while the throne the people chose sits at Gibeah obsessing over David's location.

Then they told David, saying, Behold, the Philistines fight against Keilah, and they rob the threshingfloors. Therefore David enquired of the LORD, saying, Shall I go and smite these Philistines? And the LORD said unto David, Go, and smite the Philistines, and save Keilah. Guidance: News reaches David's wilderness camp: Philistine raiders are looting the threshing floors at Keilah (a fortified town about three miles south of Adullam) during the spring harvest, when grain was at its most vulnerable. Keilah is not David's town; it is not even in his immediate territory of need. But it is Israelite, and it is being plundered. David's instinct is striking — he asks God whether to intervene. The man whom Saul calls a rebel is in fact the one acting like a king: a Spirit-anointed leader who turns to the LORD rather than to his own appetites for the next move. The inquiry probably uses Abiathar's ephod (just arrived in chapter 22; the Urim and Thummim oracle gave yes/no answers). The LORD answers: 'Go, and smite the Philistines, and save Keilah.' Note that David, in exile and outlawed, is being commissioned by God to do what Saul, the official king of Israel, is not doing. The throne God has chosen is already at work, while the throne the people chose sits at Gibeah obsessing over David's location.

And David's men said unto him, Behold, we be afraid here in Judah: how much more then if we come to Keilah against the armies of the Philistines? Then David enquired of the LORD yet again. And the LORD answered him and said, Arise, go down to Keilah; for I will deliver the Philistines into thine hand. Guidance: David's men push back. The argument is reasonable: 'we are already afraid here in Judah (where Saul keeps hunting us); how much more if we come to Keilah against the armies of the Philistines?' They are being asked to fight a war while being chased by another. David's response is one of the chapter's quietest leadership moments. He does not pull rank. He does not preach. He goes back to the LORD and asks again. The second inquiry is not for David's benefit — he already had his answer — but for the men's benefit. The LORD answers again, this time with the added assurance: 'I will deliver the Philistines into thine hand.' Sometimes a leader's job is to take a question to God twice on behalf of frightened followers, even when he himself is settled. Confirming guidance for the team is part of pastoring the team into obedience.

And David's men said unto him, Behold, we be afraid here in Judah: how much more then if we come to Keilah against the armies of the Philistines? Then David enquired of the LORD yet again. And the LORD answered him and said, Arise, go down to Keilah; for I will deliver the Philistines into thine hand. Guidance: David's men push back. The argument is reasonable: 'we are already afraid here in Judah (where Saul keeps hunting us); how much more if we come to Keilah against the armies of the Philistines?' They are being asked to fight a war while being chased by another. David's response is one of the chapter's quietest leadership moments. He does not pull rank. He does not preach. He goes back to the LORD and asks again. The second inquiry is not for David's benefit — he already had his answer — but for the men's benefit. The LORD answers again, this time with the added assurance: 'I will deliver the Philistines into thine hand.' Sometimes a leader's job is to take a question to God twice on behalf of frightened followers, even when he himself is settled. Confirming guidance for the team is part of pastoring the team into obedience.

So David and his men went to Keilah, and fought with the Philistines, and brought away their cattle, and smote them with a great slaughter. So David saved the inhabitants of Keilah. Guidance: One verse, total victory. David's men march on Keilah, engage the Philistines, drive off their livestock, and slaughter them. The narrator's final clause is the chapter's first headline: 'So David saved the inhabitants of Keilah.' The verb 'saved' (yasha) is the kingship verb — what God did through the judges, what Saul was supposed to do in chapter 8. David, in exile, does the saving the throne was supposed to do. This is the kind of detail that quietly tells you who the LORD has chosen. The man who acts as a king while still hunted as a rebel is already, in God's accounting, the king. The throne is just slow to catch up to the substance.

And it came to pass, when Abiathar the son of Ahimelech fled to David to Keilah, that he came down with an ephod in his hand. Guidance: A narrative footnote slipped in here to clarify the previous oracle-asking. When Abiathar fled the Nob massacre (22:20), he came down to Keilah carrying an ephod — the high-priestly garment containing the Urim and Thummim, the only sanctioned divinatory tool in Israel. The verse clarifies what is now permanently true: David has the priestly oracle. Saul, at Gibeah, has lost it. The official king of Israel has been cut off from the means of asking the LORD a question; the exile in the wilderness has the ephod at his side. This is one of the chapter's most quiet but theologically devastating reversals. The presence of God (Spirit, on David since 16:13), the prophet of God (Gad, on David since 22:5), and the priestly oracle of God (now on David through Abiathar) — all three lines of mediation with the LORD have migrated to David's camp. Saul has the spear; David has the LORD.

And it was told Saul that David was come to Keilah. And Saul said, God hath delivered him into mine hand; for he is shut in, by entering into a town that hath gates and bars. And Saul called all the people together to war, to go down to Keilah, to besiege David and his men. Guidance: Saul gets the news, and the very first words out of his mouth are theological: 'God hath delivered him into mine hand.' He is now in the habit of reading every event as divine endorsement of his hunt. The reasoning is geographically plausible — Keilah is a walled town; David has walked into a trap of his own design. But Saul's theological reading is dead wrong. The verse is a brutal portrait of how easily one's view of God becomes a mirror of one's wishes. The same Saul who could not hear the prophet's word for years can now confidently quote God on his own side. 'God hath delivered him' said by the man God has already rejected is the most ironic sentence in the chapter. Then Saul mobilizes the whole army to besiege Keilah. The king who would not muster troops to fight Philistines at Keilah will muster everyone to kill a fellow Israelite there. The mobilization itself is the moral diagnosis.

And it was told Saul that David was come to Keilah. And Saul said, God hath delivered him into mine hand; for he is shut in, by entering into a town that hath gates and bars. And Saul called all the people together to war, to go down to Keilah, to besiege David and his men. Guidance: Saul gets the news, and the very first words out of his mouth are theological: 'God hath delivered him into mine hand.' He is now in the habit of reading every event as divine endorsement of his hunt. The reasoning is geographically plausible — Keilah is a walled town; David has walked into a trap of his own design. But Saul's theological reading is dead wrong. The verse is a brutal portrait of how easily one's view of God becomes a mirror of one's wishes. The same Saul who could not hear the prophet's word for years can now confidently quote God on his own side. 'God hath delivered him' said by the man God has already rejected is the most ironic sentence in the chapter. Then Saul mobilizes the whole army to besiege Keilah. The king who would not muster troops to fight Philistines at Keilah will muster everyone to kill a fellow Israelite there. The mobilization itself is the moral diagnosis.

And David knew that Saul secretly practised mischief against him; and he said to Abiathar the priest, Bring hither the ephod. Then said David, O LORD God of Israel, thy servant hath certainly heard that Saul seeketh to come to Keilah, to destroy the city for my sake. Will the men of Keilah deliver me up into his hand? will Saul come down, as thy servant hath heard? O LORD God of Israel, I beseech thee, tell thy servant. And the LORD said, He will come down. Then said David, Will the men of Keilah deliver me and my men into the hand of Saul? And the LORD said, They will deliver thee up. Then David and his men, which were about six hundred, arose and departed out of Keilah, and went whithersoever they could go. And it was told Saul that David was escaped from Keilah; and he forbare to go forth. Guidance: David learns Saul is mustering and asks two precise questions through the ephod. (1) 'Will Saul come down?' — yes. (2) 'Will the men of Keilah deliver me up?' — yes. The detail is stunning. The men of Keilah are the very people David just saved from the Philistines. They will hand him over to Saul to save their own town from siege. Notice the candor of the question David is willing to ask: he is prepared to know that the people he just delivered will betray him for their own self-preservation. He does not romanticize the rescue; he asks God plainly what they will do under pressure. The LORD answers plainly. This is one of the great instances of divine guidance through hypothetical knowledge — God reveals to David not just what *will* happen but what *would* happen if David stayed. (Compare Jesus' woe to Chorazin in Matt 11:21 — God knows hypotheticals.) David acts on the warning, leaves Keilah with his now-600 men (the band has grown from 400 in 22:2), and moves to higher country. Saul, hearing of the escape, calls off the muster. The chapter has shown us a fugitive king who saves a city, then is told God's verdict about how that city would have repaid him, and quietly leaves before the betrayal can occur. Sometimes faith looks like leaving a place you saved, on God's word, before the rescue is ever thanked.

And David abode in the wilderness in strong holds, and remained in a mountain in the wilderness of Ziph. And Saul sought him every day, but God delivered him not into his hand. Guidance: A summary verse covering an indefinite period. David moves from Keilah into the wilderness of Ziph — rugged hill country south of Hebron, full of caves and ravines, with hostile locals (we are about to meet them). 'Saul sought him every day, but God delivered him not into his hand.' Two parallel clauses with the same form, opposite outcomes. Saul's pursuit is unrelenting; God's protection is also unrelenting. The verse sets the pattern that will define the wilderness years: human persistence in evil against divine persistence in protection. The phrase 'God delivered him not into his hand' is the precise negative of Saul's confident 'God hath delivered him into mine hand' in v7. The verse functions as the narrator's quiet correction of Saul's theology. Saul says God is on his side daily; the narrator says God is not. The man who manages to misread God's hand for years at a stretch is a special category of human catastrophe.

And David saw that Saul was come out to seek his life: and David was in the wilderness of Ziph in a wood. And Jonathan Saul's son arose, and went to David into the wood, and strengthened his hand in God. Guidance: David sees what is coming — Saul has personally come out to hunt him. The wilderness pressure is now unrelenting. Then a small miracle of friendship: 'Jonathan Saul's son arose, and went to David into the wood, and strengthened his hand in God.' Jonathan, the heir to Saul's throne, slips away from his father's camp and finds David in the woods — somehow, against the active manhunt, without giving up David's location. This is the last meeting between Jonathan and David in scripture. The phrase 'strengthened his hand in God' (Hebrew chizzaq beyad-elohim) is one of the most beautiful in the Old Testament. Jonathan does not bring soldiers, food, or a plan. He brings God-talk. He sits with David in the woods and speaks the LORD's promises over him until David's grip on the truth firms up. Sometimes the deepest pastoral work between friends is not solving problems but simply re-stating, in person, what God has already said. Jonathan, who could have brought everything else, brings the one thing that matters. The friends do not need many more conversations after this; this one will sustain David for the rest of the wilderness years and into the throne.

And he said unto him, Fear not: for the hand of Saul my father shall not find thee; and thou shalt be king over Israel, and I shall be next unto thee; and that also Saul my father knoweth. And they two made a covenant before the LORD: and David abode in the wood, and Jonathan went to his house. Guidance: Jonathan's speech is one of the most extraordinary in scripture. 'Fear not: for the hand of Saul my father shall not find thee.' He speaks as a prophet rather than a politician; he is naming what God will do. 'And thou shalt be king over Israel, and I shall be next unto thee.' Jonathan, the legal heir, formally accepts second place. He is not just resigned to David's reign; he is volunteering for the role of David's chief advisor. 'And that also Saul my father knoweth' — the most chilling note. Jonathan tells David that his father knows the kingdom is going to David, and is fighting God's settled decree anyway. Saul's tragedy is now fully named: he opposes a transition he himself believes is divinely ordered. The two men renew their covenant — the third covenant scene between them (18:3, 20:16, 23:18) — and Jonathan returns to the camp of the man trying to kill them both. The picture of Jonathan walking back to Gibeah, having just told the chosen king 'I will be second to you,' is one of the noblest in scripture. He returns to his father's losing house out of filial loyalty, knowing how it will end. Some forms of obedience to flesh and blood are also a kind of martyrdom; Jonathan will die fighting beside his father on Gilboa, having already given away the throne that could have been his.

Then came up the Ziphites to Saul to Gibeah, saying, Doth not David hide himself with us in strong holds in the wood, in the hill of Hachilah, which is on the south of Jeshimon? Now therefore, O king, come down according to all the desire of thy soul to come down; and our part shall be to deliver him into the king's hand. Guidance: The men of Ziph — the very tribe whose land David is in — make the long trip up to Gibeah to inform on him. The verse's geography is precise (the hill of Hachilah, south of Jeshimon, in the wood — locations a guide could pinpoint). The Ziphites volunteer betrayal; David has not even passed through their towns harming anyone, and they hand him over for whatever favor they can extract from the throne. The line 'come down according to all the desire of thy soul' is sycophantic flattery — they tell the king they will indulge his every wish. And they promise to be the delivery agents themselves. There is no nobler enemy than a wronged neighbor; there is no more contemptible one than a fellow Israelite who sells you to a king for political favor. David will write Psalm 54 about this very incident — 'Save me, O God, by thy name… for strangers are risen up against me, and oppressors seek after my soul' — and the prayer ends in praise of the LORD's deliverance even before that deliverance arrives.

And Saul said, Blessed be ye of the LORD; for ye have compassion on me. Go, I pray you, prepare yet, and know and see his place where his haunt is, and who hath seen him there: for it is told me that he dealeth very subtilly. See therefore, and take knowledge of all the lurking places where he hideth himself, and come ye again to me with the certainty, and I will go with you: and it shall come to pass, if he be in the land, that I will search him out throughout all the thousands of Judah. Guidance: 'Blessed be ye of the LORD; for ye have compassion on me.' Saul invokes the LORD's blessing on men who have just promised to help him commit a judicial murder. This is one of the chapter's most blasphemous lines. The king who could not destroy Agag invokes the LORD's blessing on Israelites who are turning over an innocent fellow-Israelite for assassination. And the language he uses — 'ye have compassion on me' — is the language Saul once used to be the people-pleaser; even his self-pity has not changed. Then he gives the Ziphites a careful reconnaissance brief: scout the place, learn the hiding spots, identify witnesses, report back with certainty. 'For it is told me that he dealeth very subtilly' — David is clever, slippery, hard to catch. Saul has accumulated enough humiliating misses (chapters 19-22) to know that ordinary military deployment will fail. He needs precise intelligence first. The king has become his own intelligence chief; the entire apparatus of state is now organized around hunting one man. 'I will search him out throughout all the thousands of Judah' — Saul will not stop. The next chapters will show that he does not stop until he runs out of life.

Saul has accumulated enough humiliating misses (chapters 19-22) to know that ordinary military deployment will fail. He needs precise intelligence first. The king has become his own intelligence chief; the entire apparatus of state is now organized around hunting one man. 'I will search him out throughout all the thousands of Judah' — Saul will not stop. The next chapters will show that he does not stop until he runs out of life.

And Saul said, Blessed be ye of the LORD; for ye have compassion on me. Go, I pray you, prepare yet, and know and see his place where his haunt is, and who hath seen him there: for it is told me that he dealeth very subtilly. See therefore, and take knowledge of all the lurking places where he hideth himself, and come ye again to me with the certainty, and I will go with you: and it shall come to pass, if he be in the land, that I will search him out throughout all the thousands of Judah. Guidance: 'Blessed be ye of the LORD; for ye have compassion on me.' Saul invokes the LORD's blessing on men who have just promised to help him commit a judicial murder. This is one of the chapter's most blasphemous lines. The king who could not destroy Agag invokes the LORD's blessing on Israelites who are turning over an innocent fellow-Israelite for assassination. And the language he uses — 'ye have compassion on me' — is the language Saul once used to be the people-pleaser; even his self-pity has not changed. Then he gives the Ziphites a careful reconnaissance brief: scout the place, learn the hiding spots, identify witnesses, report back with certainty. 'For it is told me that he dealeth very subtilly' — David is clever, slippery, hard to catch. Saul has accumulated enough humiliating misses (chapters 19-22) to know that ordinary military deployment will fail. He needs precise intelligence first. The king has become his own intelligence chief; the entire apparatus of state is now organized around hunting one man. 'I will search him out throughout all the thousands of Judah' — Saul will not stop. The next chapters will show that he does not stop until he runs out of life.

And they arose, and went to Ziph before Saul: but David and his men were in the wilderness of Maon, in the plain on the south of Jeshimon. Saul also and his men went to seek him. And they told David: wherefore he came down into a rock, and abode in the wilderness of Maon. And when Saul heard that, he pursued after David in the wilderness of Maon. Guidance: The Ziphites go ahead to reconnoiter. David has already moved south to the wilderness of Maon — a more arid, less-populated stretch toward the Negev, around Carmel of Judah (not the famous northern Carmel; another town with the same name). Probably someone in David's network (his intelligence is good; the wilderness years are teaching him the art of advance warning) tipped him off. Saul, hearing of the move, follows. The chase is intimate now — Saul and his men, David and his men, two small forces tracking each other through dry brush and limestone outcrops. The narrator's pacing tightens. We are about to read one of the closest near-captures in the wilderness years.

And they told David: wherefore he came down into a rock, and abode in the wilderness of Maon. And when Saul heard that, he pursued after David in the wilderness of Maon. The chase is intimate now — Saul and his men, David and his men, two small forces tracking each other through dry brush and limestone outcrops. The narrator's pacing tightens. We are about to read one of the closest near-captures in the wilderness years.

And Saul went on this side of the mountain, and David and his men on that side of the mountain: and David made haste to get away for fear of Saul; for Saul and his men compassed David and his men round about to take them. But there came a messenger unto Saul, saying, Haste thee, and come; for the Philistines have invaded the land. Wherefore Saul returned from pursuing after David, and went against the Philistines. Therefore they called that place Sela-hammahlekoth. Guidance: The visual is cinematic. The two forces are on opposite sides of the same mountain, working in opposite directions around it; David is racing to keep the ridge between him and Saul, but Saul's men are pincering. 'Saul and his men compassed David and his men round about to take them' — the Hebrew implies Saul's net is closing. Then — a messenger. Philistine raid into Israelite territory; Saul must abandon the chase and defend the country. The timing is so precise that one cannot read it as anything but providence. The God who knew the hypothetical of the Keilah betrayal also knew when the Philistines would invade. The deliverance is named: 'Sela-hammahlekoth' — 'the Rock of Separation' or 'the Rock of Slipping Away.' The name will be remembered. Generations of fugitives in Judah will know that mountain as the place where the LORD's anointed was about to fall and didn't. Faith in pursuit-season clings to the geography of God's previous rescues. He gave you a rock once; He will not abandon you on the next mountain.

And David went up from thence, and dwelt in strong holds at En-gedi. Guidance: One closing verse. En-gedi (literally 'the spring of the kid') is the famous oasis on the western shore of the Dead Sea — sheer limestone cliffs riddled with caves, fed by a year-round spring, surrounded by some of the most defensible terrain in the entire region. David and his men move there. The Hebrew word for 'strong holds' is metsudoth — fortresses, in this case natural ones. En-gedi is the perfect base of operations: water, caves for shelter, cliffs for security, and bare rocky terrain that makes large troop movements nearly impossible. The chapter's last move is a wisdom move; David takes the geography God's deliverance has given him and settles where he can be effectively unfindable. The next chapter will open with Saul pursuing David here. The chase resumes; the rest of the book will continue in this rhythm — pursue, rescue, settle, pursue again. But the resting place at En-gedi has its own significance. David will write some of the most quoted Psalms about thirst, springs, and the rock that hides him from here. 'As the hart panteth after the water brooks, so panteth my soul after thee, O God' — written, perhaps, by a man who watched the wild ibex come down to drink at En-gedi's spring each evening, and saw in their thirst the picture of his own.
